notes.note: Trebianus was a Pompeian who remained in arms beyond the point when Cicero (and others) made their peace with Caesar; he was apparently still in exile or awaiting formal pardon when Cicero wrote this letter (c. August 46 BC), after Caesar's African campaign.

Titus Pomponius Atticus, Cicero's closest friend and prolific correspondent; a wealthy eques who stayed scrupulously neutral in the civil wars and acted as a channel of communication and goodwill for friends on all sides.

Cicero is careful not to name Caesar directly here; his claim to influence through Caesar's inner circle — men he had known for decades before the civil war — is a recurring theme in the consolation letters of Book 6.

notes.anchor: your own consciousness of what you have done and meant
notes.note: The Stoic (and broadly philosophical) consolation that the testimony of one's own conscience is the truest comfort in adversity; Cicero employs the same argument in the Tusculan Disputations and elsewhere in the exile-consolation letters.

notes.anchor: he himself, who has the most power, seems to me daily to drift back toward equity
notes.note: Cicero's guarded way of saying Caesar is becoming more merciful over time; 'drift back toward equity and toward the nature of things' echoes Stoic vocabulary of natural order (rerum natura) and hints that arbitrary one-man rule is inherently unstable.
